  • 1 Overview
  • 2 Getting Started
  • 3 Plotting Ternary Diagram with R
  • 4 References

Hands-on Exercise 5a: Creating Ternary Plot with R

Author

Andy Tai

Published

May 9, 2026

Modified

May 9, 2026

1 Overview

Ternary plots are graphical tools used to visualise the distribution and variation of compositional data made up of three components. Examples include the proportions of young, economically active, and elderly populations, or the composition of sand, silt, and clay in soil samples. The plot is represented as a triangle scaled from 0 to 1 along each side, where each side corresponds to one of the three components. A data point is positioned such that its perpendicular distance to each side reflects the value of the respective component.

In this hands-on exercise, we will learn how to create ternary plots programmatically in R to analyse and visualise the population structure of Singapore.

The exercise is organised into four main steps:

  1. Install and load the tidyverse and ggtern packages.
  2. Create three new measures using the mutate() function from the dplyr package.
  3. Construct a static ternary plot using the ggtern() function from the ggtern package.
  4. Create an interactive ternary plot using the plot_ly() function from the Plotly R package.

2 Getting Started

  • Installing & Launching R Packages
  • Importing Data
  • Preparing Data

In this exercise, we will use two main R packages:

  • ggtern, an extension of ggplot2 designed specifically for creating ternary diagrams. We will use this package to build static ternary plots.
  • Plotly R, an R package for developing interactive web-based visualisations using Plotly’s JavaScript graphing library, plotly.js. We will use the ggplotly() function to convert ggplot2 graphics into interactive Plotly objects.
Show the code 
pacman::p_load(plotly, tidyverse, ggtern, tricolore, gganimate)

The original data is obtained from Singapore Residents by Planning AreaSubzone, Age Group, Sex and Type of Dwelling, June 2000-2025 off the Singstat website.

Show the code 
#Reading the data into R environment
pop_data <- read_csv("data/respopagesextod2025.csv")

The code chunk below performs several tasks:

  • Uses as.character() to convert the Year variable into the character data type.
  • Uses spread() to transform key-value pairs into multiple columns, similar to a simpler version of pivot_wider().
  • Syntax: spread(data, key, value), where:
    • data refers to the data frame,
    • key specifies the column whose values will become new column names, and
    • value specifies the column containing the values to populate the new columns.
  • Uses the mutate() function from the dplyr package to create three new measures: young, active, and old.
Show the code 
agpop_mutated <- pop_data %>%
  filter(Sex == "Males") %>%
  spread(AG, Pop) %>%
  mutate(
    YOUNG  = rowSums(select(., `0_to_4`, `5_to_9`, `10_to_14`, `15_to_19`, `20_to_24`), na.rm = TRUE),
    ACTIVE = rowSums(select(., `25_to_29`, `30_to_34`, `35_to_39`, `40_to_44`, `45_to_49`,`50_to_54`, `55_to_59`, `60_to_64`), na.rm = TRUE),
    OLD    = rowSums(select(., `65_to_69`, `70_to_74`, `75_to_79`, `80_to_84`, 
                               `85_to_89`, `90_and_over`), na.rm = TRUE),
    TOTAL  = YOUNG + ACTIVE + OLD
  ) %>%
  filter(Time == 2025) %>%
  filter(TOTAL > 0)

head(agpop_mutated)
# A tibble: 6 × 28
  PA         SZ      Sex   TOD    Time `0_to_4` `10_to_14` `15_to_19` `20_to_24`
  <chr>      <chr>   <chr> <chr> <dbl>    <dbl>      <dbl>      <dbl>      <dbl>
1 Ang Mo Kio Ang Mo… Males Cond…  2025       30         80         70         80
2 Ang Mo Kio Ang Mo… Males HDB …  2025        0         10         10          0
3 Ang Mo Kio Ang Mo… Males HDB …  2025       10         10         10         20
4 Ang Mo Kio Ang Mo… Males HDB …  2025       10         50         50         40
5 Ang Mo Kio Cheng … Males HDB …  2025       20         20         10         30
6 Ang Mo Kio Cheng … Males HDB …  2025      120        170        210        260
# ℹ 19 more variables: `25_to_29` <dbl>, `30_to_34` <dbl>, `35_to_39` <dbl>,
#   `40_to_44` <dbl>, `45_to_49` <dbl>, `5_to_9` <dbl>, `50_to_54` <dbl>,
#   `55_to_59` <dbl>, `60_to_64` <dbl>, `65_to_69` <dbl>, `70_to_74` <dbl>,
#   `75_to_79` <dbl>, `80_to_84` <dbl>, `85_to_89` <dbl>, `90_and_over` <dbl>,
#   YOUNG <dbl>, ACTIVE <dbl>, OLD <dbl>, TOTAL <dbl>

3 Plotting Ternary Diagram with R

  • (a) Basic Plot
  • (b) Subplots + tricolore
  • (c) Interactive
  • (d) gganimate
  • (e) Animated

Here, we use ggtern() function of ggtern package to create a simple ternary plot. We also style it with color and title.

Show the code 
ggtern(data = agpop_mutated,
       aes(x = YOUNG, y = ACTIVE, z = OLD, colour = YOUNG)) +
  geom_point() +
  scale_colour_viridis_c(option = "plasma", name = "Young Population") +
  labs(title = "Population Structure, 2025") +
  theme_bw() +
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(hjust = 0.5, face = "bold"),
    legend.background = element_rect(colour = "grey93", fill = "grey93"),
    plot.background = element_rect(fill = "grey93", colour = "grey93")
  )

The following code filter the data for male and female.

Show the code 
library(tricolore)
library(cowplot)

agpop_male <- pop_data %>%
  filter(Sex == "Males") %>%
  spread(AG, Pop) %>%
  mutate(
    YOUNG  = rowSums(select(., `0_to_4`, `5_to_9`, `10_to_14`, `15_to_19`, `20_to_24`), na.rm = TRUE),
    ACTIVE = rowSums(select(., `25_to_29`, `30_to_34`, `35_to_39`, `40_to_44`, `45_to_49`, `50_to_54`, `55_to_59`, `60_to_64`), na.rm = TRUE),
    OLD    = rowSums(select(., `65_to_69`, `70_to_74`, `75_to_79`, `80_to_84`,
                               `85_to_89`, `90_and_over`), na.rm = TRUE),
    TOTAL  = YOUNG + ACTIVE + OLD
  ) %>%
  filter(Time == 2025, TOTAL > 0)

agpop_male$tc_color <- Tricolore(agpop_male, p1 = "YOUNG", p2 = "ACTIVE", p3 = "OLD")$color

agpop_female <- pop_data %>%
  filter(Sex == "Females") %>%
  spread(AG, Pop) %>%
  mutate(
    YOUNG  = rowSums(select(., `0_to_4`, `5_to_9`, `10_to_14`, `15_to_19`, `20_to_24`), na.rm = TRUE),
    ACTIVE = rowSums(select(., `25_to_29`, `30_to_34`, `35_to_39`, `40_to_44`, `45_to_49`, `50_to_54`, `55_to_59`, `60_to_64`), na.rm = TRUE),
    OLD    = rowSums(select(., `65_to_69`, `70_to_74`, `75_to_79`, `80_to_84`,
                               `85_to_89`, `90_and_over`), na.rm = TRUE),
    TOTAL  = YOUNG + ACTIVE + OLD
  ) %>%
  filter(Time == 2025, TOTAL > 0)

agpop_female$tc_color <- Tricolore(agpop_female, p1 = "YOUNG", p2 = "ACTIVE", p3 = "OLD")$color

Normalise proportions and regenerate Tricolore colors

Show the code 
agpop_male <- agpop_male %>%
  mutate(
    YOUNG_P  = YOUNG / TOTAL,
    ACTIVE_P = ACTIVE / TOTAL,
    OLD_P    = OLD / TOTAL
  )

agpop_female <- agpop_female %>%
  mutate(
    YOUNG_P  = YOUNG / TOTAL,
    ACTIVE_P = ACTIVE / TOTAL,
    OLD_P    = OLD / TOTAL
  )

agpop_male$tc_color   <- Tricolore(agpop_male,   p1 = "YOUNG_P", p2 = "ACTIVE_P", p3 = "OLD_P")$rgb
agpop_female$tc_color <- Tricolore(agpop_female, p1 = "YOUNG_P", p2 = "ACTIVE_P", p3 = "OLD_P")$rgb

Next, we create the ternary diagrams for male and female side-by-side using layout-ncol=2

Show the code 
male_colors <- agpop_male$tc_color   #<< extract first

ggtern(data = agpop_male,
       aes(x = YOUNG, y = ACTIVE, z = OLD)) +
  geom_point(color = male_colors,    #<< plain vector
             size = 2, alpha = 0.8) +
  labs(title = "Males, 2025", x = "Young", y = "Active", z = "Old") +
  theme_bw() +
  theme(plot.title = element_text(hjust = 0.5, face = "bold"))
female_colors <- agpop_female$tc_color  #<< extract first

ggtern(data = agpop_female,
       aes(x = YOUNG, y = ACTIVE, z = OLD)) +
  geom_point(color = female_colors,     #<< plain vector
             size = 2, alpha = 0.8) +
  labs(title = "Females, 2025", x = "Young", y = "Active", z = "Old") +
  theme_bw() +
  theme(plot.title = element_text(hjust = 0.5, face = "bold"))
Show the code 
paxis <- function(txt) {
  list(
    title = txt,
    tickformat = ".0%",
    tickfont = list(size = 10),
    titlefont = list(size = 12),
    gridcolor = "lightgrey",
    linecolor = "black",
    linewidth = 1
  )
}

ternaryAxes <- list(
  aaxis = paxis("Young"),
  baxis = paxis("Economically Active"),
  caxis = paxis("Aged")
)

plot_ly() %>%
  add_trace(
    data = agpop_male,
    a = ~YOUNG_P, b = ~ACTIVE_P, c = ~OLD_P,
    color = I("steelblue"),
    type = "scatterternary",
    mode = "markers",
    marker = list(size = 6, opacity = 0.7),
    text = ~paste("PA:", PA, "<br>SZ:", SZ),
    hoverinfo = "text",
    subplot = "ternary"
  ) %>%
  add_trace(
    data = agpop_female,
    a = ~YOUNG_P, b = ~ACTIVE_P, c = ~OLD_P,
    color = I("tomato"),
    type = "scatterternary",
    mode = "markers",
    marker = list(size = 6, opacity = 0.7),
    text = ~paste("PA:", PA, "<br>SZ:", SZ),
    hoverinfo = "text",
    subplot = "ternary2"
  ) %>%
  layout(
    title = list(
      text = "Population Structure by Gender, 2025",
      font = list(size = 14, family = "Arial")
    ),
    
    ## ----- ADJUST THE GAP BETWEEN THE 2 TRIANGLES ---
ternary  = c(ternaryAxes, list(domain = list(x = c(0.05, 0.42), y = c(0, 0.9)))),
ternary2 = c(ternaryAxes, list(domain = list(x = c(0.62, 0.97), y = c(0, 0.9)))),
    
    ## ----------
annotations = list(list(text = "Males, 2025",   x = 0.15, y = 0.98, xref = "paper", yref = "paper", showarrow = FALSE, font = list(size = 13),
bgcolor = "white", bordercolor = "black", borderwidth = 1.5),
list(text = "Females, 2025", x = 0.82, y = 0.98, xref = "paper", yref = "paper",
showarrow = FALSE, font = list(size = 13),
bgcolor = "white", bordercolor = "black", borderwidth = 1.5)),
paper_bgcolor = "white",
showlegend = FALSE,
margin = list(t = 80, r = 100, l = 80, b = 60)  #<< right margin to prevent truncation
  )

Here, we shall use gganimate() to create an animated plot.

First, let’s filter the data. Since this dataset has only one year of data (2025), we shall animate using ‘Type of Dwelling (TOD)’ column.

Show the code 
agpop_animated <- pop_data %>%
  spread(AG, Pop) %>%                                    #<< spread first
  mutate(across(`0_to_4`:`90_and_over`, ~replace_na(., 0))) %>%
  group_by(PA, SZ, TOD, Time) %>%
  summarise(
    across(`0_to_4`:`90_and_over`, sum),                 #<< then sum by group
    .groups = "drop"
  ) %>%
  mutate(
    YOUNG  = rowSums(select(., `0_to_4`, `5_to_9`, `10_to_14`, `15_to_19`, `20_to_24`), na.rm = TRUE),
    ACTIVE = rowSums(select(., `25_to_29`, `30_to_34`, `35_to_39`, `40_to_44`, `45_to_49`,`50_to_54`, `55_to_59`, `60_to_64`), na.rm = TRUE),
    OLD    = rowSums(select(., `65_to_69`, `70_to_74`, `75_to_79`, `80_to_84`,
`85_to_89`, `90_and_over`), na.rm = TRUE),
    TOTAL  = YOUNG + ACTIVE + OLD
  ) %>%
  filter(Time == 2025, TOTAL > 0)

Next we animate the plot

Show the code 
library(magick)

tod_list <- unique(agpop_animated$TOD)

frames <- lapply(tod_list, function(tod) {
  df <- agpop_animated %>% filter(TOD == tod)
  
  p <- ggtern(data = df,
              aes(x = YOUNG, y = ACTIVE, z = OLD,
                  size = TOTAL, color = PA)) +
    geom_point(alpha = 0.7, show.legend = FALSE) +
    scale_color_viridis_d(option = "plasma") +
    scale_size(range = c(2, 15)) +
    scale_L_continuous("Young\n(0-24)") +
    scale_T_continuous("Working Age\n(25-64)") +
    scale_R_continuous("Aged\n(65+)") +
    labs(
      title = "Singapore Population Age Structure by Dwelling Type, 2025",
      subtitle = paste("Dwelling Type:", tod)
    ) +
    theme_bw() +
    theme(
      tern.axis.arrow.show = TRUE,
      tern.axis.text    = element_text(size = 8, colour = "grey20"),
      tern.axis.title.L = element_text(hjust = 0.2, vjust = 0.7, angle = -60),
      tern.axis.title.R = element_text(hjust = 0.8, vjust = 0.6, angle = 60),
      plot.title    = element_text(hjust = 0.5, face = "bold"),
      plot.subtitle = element_text(hjust = 0.5, face = "bold"),
      legend.position = "none"
    )
  
  tmp <- tempfile(fileext = ".png")
  ggsave(tmp, p, width = 7, height = 6, dpi = 100)
  image_read(tmp)
})

animation <- image_join(frames)
image_animate(animation, fps = 1)

Lastly, here’s the chart using plotly animation.

Show the code 
td3 <- plot_ly(agpop_animated,
               a = ~ACTIVE,
               b = ~YOUNG,
               c = ~OLD,
               frame = ~TOD,
               mode = "markers",
               size = ~TOTAL,
               color = ~PA,
               type = "scatterternary",
               text = ~paste("Young:", YOUNG,
                             "<br>Economically Active:", ACTIVE,
                             "<br>Aged:", OLD,
                             "<br>Subzone:", SZ,
                             "<br>Planning Area:", PA),
               hoverinfo = "text",
               marker = list(
                 symbol = "circle",
                 opacity = 0.8,
                 sizemode = "diameter",
                 sizeref = 2,
                 line = list(width = 2, color = "#FFFFFF")
               )) %>%
  layout(
    title = list(
      text = "Demographic Composition of Singapore by Dwelling Type, 2025",
      font = list(size = 14, family = "Arial"),
      x = 0.5,
      y = 0.98                                      #<< push title up
    ),
    ternary = list(
      aaxis = list(title = "Economically Active",
                   tickformat = ".0%",
                   gridcolor = "lightgrey",
                   linecolor = "black"),
      baxis = list(title = "Young",
                   tickformat = ".0%",
                   gridcolor = "lightgrey",
                   linecolor = "black"),
      caxis = list(title = "Aged",
                   tickformat = ".0%",
                   gridcolor = "lightgrey",
                   linecolor = "black"),
      domain = list(x = c(0, 1), y = c(0, 0.88))   #<< shrink ternary down
    ),
    paper_bgcolor = "white",
    plot_bgcolor  = "white",
    margin = list(t = 80)                           #<< top margin
  )

td3

4 References

  • Kam, T.S. (2023). Creating Ternary Plot with R.
